title: Pivot or Persist Decision
prompt: My SaaS product has 200 users after 12 months, $1,800 MRR, 4% monthly churn. I've spent $40K of personal savings. Users love it but I can't grow past word-of-mouth. A competitor just raised $5M. Do I pivot, persist, or sell? Analyze all three options with realistic numbers, second-order consequences, and a final recommendation I can act on this week.

title: Acquisition Channel Prioritization
prompt: I run a B2B analytics tool with $15K MRR. I have $3,000/month for growth. Rank these channels by expected ROI for my specific situation: (1) Google Ads, (2) LinkedIn content, (3) cold email, (4) SEO, (5) Product Hunt launch, (6) partnership with a complementary tool. For each: estimated CAC, time to first results, scalability ceiling, and what specifically I should do in week 1.

title: Contradictory Expert Advice
prompt: I'm getting contradictory advice about my e-commerce store. My marketing consultant says 'double your ad spend — your ROAS of 2.5x is healthy.' My accountant says 'your cash conversion cycle is 45 days, you'll run out of cash in 3 months at this burn rate.' My mentor says 'focus on organic and stop paid entirely.' Who is right? Can all three be partially correct? Give me a unified strategy that addresses all three perspectives without dismissing any. Current numbers: $80K/month revenue, $32K ad spend, $15K inventory, $12K overhead, $25K in the bank.

title: Data Interpretation Conflict
prompt: My product analytics show contradictory signals: (1) NPS is 72 (excellent), (2) 30-day retention is only 35% (poor), (3) support tickets per user are declining, (4) feature usage is concentrated — 80% of users only use 2 of 12 features, (5) users who DO retain have 95% satisfaction. Reconcile these signals. What is actually happening? What should I build next? What should I kill? Don't just describe the data — give me a specific product roadmap based on this analysis.

title: 90-Day Execution Plan with Dependencies
prompt: I just got approved to build an AI-powered internal tool at my company (500 employees, healthcare industry). Budget: $50K. Team: me (PM) + 1 senior developer + access to a part-time designer. Build a 90-day plan with weekly milestones, dependency chains (what blocks what), risk mitigation at each stage, and go/no-go decision points. The tool should auto-categorize and route patient feedback from 3 channels (email, phone transcripts, survey) to the right department. Include compliance considerations (HIPAA).

title: Launch Sequencing Under Constraints
prompt: I'm launching a marketplace connecting freelance accountants with small businesses. I have both sides of the chicken-and-egg problem. I have $10K, no team, and 3 months before I need to show traction to an angel investor. Design the exact launch sequence: which side to seed first, how to fake supply/demand initially, what the MVP looks like, the first 10 customers acquisition playbook, and the metrics I need to hit by month 3 to be fundable. Be brutally specific — no 'leverage your network' generics.

title: Competitor Moat Analysis
prompt: Analyze Calendly's competitive moat as of 2026. Cover: (1) what makes them defensible, (2) where their moat is weakest, (3) which competitor (Cal.com, SavvyCal, TidyCal, or a newcomer) has the best chance of taking significant market share and why, (4) if you had $2M to build a Calendly competitor, what specific wedge would you use to enter the market, and (5) what Calendly will likely do in the next 12 months to strengthen their position.

title: Market Entry Timing Analysis
prompt: Is now (April 2026) the right time to launch an AI-powered contract review tool for SMBs? Analyze: (1) what existing players are doing and where they're weak, (2) whether the market is too early, too late, or just right, (3) the regulatory environment for AI in legal tech, (4) what the ideal ICP looks like and their willingness to pay, (5) the 3 biggest reasons this could fail and the 3 biggest reasons it could succeed. I need an honest assessment, not encouragement.

title: Pricing Page Optimization
prompt: Redesign my SaaS pricing page. Current: Free ($0), Pro ($29/mo), Enterprise (contact us). Free tier has 70% of signups and <1% conversion to paid. Pro has everything most users need. Enterprise gets almost no inbound. Specific problems: no urgency to upgrade, free tier too generous, Pro price may be too low or too high. Design the new pricing page: tier names, prices, feature allocation, psychological anchoring, what to put behind the paywall, annual discount strategy, and the exact CTA copy for each tier. Include the logic behind every decision.

title: Dynamic Pricing Model Design
prompt: Design a dynamic pricing model for a food delivery service competing with DoorDash in a mid-size US city. Variables to factor: time of day, demand surge, weather, restaurant distance, driver availability, order size, customer loyalty tier, competitor pricing. For each variable: explain the pricing logic, give specific multiplier ranges, identify customer perception risks, and describe guardrails to prevent backlash. Also design the customer-facing explanation (how you communicate variable pricing without losing trust).

title: Microservices Migration Decision
prompt: My monolithic Django app serves 50K DAU, has 180K lines of code, and deployment takes 45 minutes. The team (6 developers) is hitting velocity problems. CTO wants microservices. I'm skeptical. Evaluate: (1) is this the right move or is a modular monolith better for our size, (2) if we do migrate, which services should be extracted first and why, (3) realistic timeline and cost for a team of 6, (4) what will break during migration and how to mitigate, (5) the honest answer about whether microservices actually improve velocity at our scale.

title: Real-Time System Cost Optimization
prompt: My real-time analytics dashboard costs $8,500/month on AWS (mostly Kinesis + Lambda + DynamoDB + CloudFront). We have 200 concurrent users max, processing ~500 events/second. This feels too expensive. Audit the likely architecture, identify where I'm overpaying, and design a cheaper alternative that maintains <2s data freshness. Include specific AWS service swaps, estimated new monthly cost, and migration risk assessment. I need actual numbers, not 'consider alternatives.'

title: Hazmat Shipment Compliance
prompt: I need to ship 4 pallets of Class 3 flammable liquids (paint thinner, 800 gallons total) from Houston TX to Phoenix AZ. What DOT regulations apply? What packaging requirements exist? What placarding is needed on the trailer? Do I need a hazmat-endorsed driver? What documentation must travel with the shipment? What insurance requirements apply? Provide a complete compliance checklist my dispatcher can use.

title: Intermodal Cost-Benefit Analysis
prompt: Compare door-to-door costs for shipping 2,000 cases of wine (40,000 lbs total, temperature-sensitive, max 75°F) from Napa Valley CA to Miami FL. Options: (A) full reefer truck direct, (B) reefer truck to Oakland rail yard → intermodal reefer to Jacksonville → reefer truck to Miami, (C) reefer truck to Port of Oakland → coastal shipping to Port of Miami → local delivery. For each: estimated cost, transit time, temperature risk, breakage risk, and insurance implications. Current diesel is $3.85/gallon.

title: Home Service Lead Machine
prompt: I'm an electrician in Tampa FL, solo operator, doing $12K/month. I want to hit $25K/month within 6 months without hiring. Design the complete lead generation system: Google Business Profile optimization (specific steps), the 5 most valuable keywords to target, a realistic content plan I can do in 2 hours/week, a review generation system, and a follow-up sequence for estimates that don't close. Include actual tools with prices. Total budget: $400/month for marketing.

title: Multi-Location Expansion Playbook
prompt: I run a successful dog grooming business with 1 location doing $35K/month. I want to open location #2. I have $60K saved. Design the expansion playbook: site selection criteria for the second location, staffing plan (how many groomers, at what pay), equipment list with costs, realistic ramp-up timeline to breakeven, marketing plan for the new location, and what systems I need to have in place before opening (booking, payroll, inventory). Include the specific financial model: monthly costs, revenue projections months 1-6, and breakeven analysis.

title: Supply Chain Disruption Response
prompt: You're the operations VP of a mid-size US furniture manufacturer. Your primary lumber supplier (60% of supply) just informed you they're shutting down in 30 days due to bankruptcy. You have 2 weeks of inventory. Design the crisis response: immediate actions (first 48 hours), supplier replacement strategy (domestic and import options with lead times), customer communication plan, production schedule adjustments, financial impact analysis, and a 90-day stabilization plan. Be specific about lumber species, typical pricing, and realistic supplier onboarding timelines.

title: Regulatory Change Impact Assessment
prompt: The EU AI Act is being enforced starting 2026. My company sells an AI-powered hiring screening tool to European companies. Assess: (1) which risk category my product falls into under the AI Act, (2) specific compliance requirements I must meet, (3) timeline and cost to become compliant, (4) what happens if I don't comply (specific penalties), (5) whether I should continue serving EU customers or exit the market, and (6) how competitors are likely responding. I need a decision framework, not a legal summary.

title: Ambiguous Client Request
prompt: A client just emailed me: 'We need to modernize our customer experience. Budget is around $200K, need it done by Q3. We've been losing customers to competitors who seem more tech-forward. Our current system is a mess — part Salesforce, part spreadsheets, part paper. We want AI involved somehow. Can you put together a proposal?' This is vague and full of hidden assumptions. Write the proposal outline AND separately write the 10 clarifying questions I should ask before writing the full proposal. For the outline, make reasonable assumptions and state them explicitly.

title: Conflicting Stakeholder Requirements
prompt: I'm a product manager and three stakeholders are pulling me in different directions for our next quarter roadmap: (1) CEO wants a flashy AI feature for investor demo (2 months of dev), (2) Head of Sales says we're losing 40% of deals because we lack SSO and audit logs (enterprise requirements), (3) Head of CS says our onboarding flow is so broken that 50% of new users churn in the first week. I have 1 senior dev and 1 junior dev for the quarter. Analyze each request: business impact, opportunity cost of NOT doing it, realistic scope with my team, and make a single recommendation with explicit reasoning for what I deprioritize and why. Include the exact conversation I should have with each stakeholder about why their request was or wasn't chosen.
